column33: At the national level, the government should increase investment in educational infrastructure by building and renovating schools and providing modern learning facilities. More qualified teachers should be recruited, properly trained, and well motivated through improved salaries and welfare packages. Adequate manpower should be ensured to reduce workload and improve teaching effectiveness. To address poverty, education should be made free or heavily subsidized, with scholarships and financial support for students from low-income families. The national curriculum should be reviewed to make learning practical, engaging, and balanced with fun, creativity, and relaxation.

column32: The most critical issue is an over-reliance on an outdated, theory-heavy curriculum. Currently, our system prioritizes rote memorization and academic "box-ticking" over actual competence. Students are graduating with high grades but lack the practical, real-world skills required to solve local problems or thrive in a modern global economy. We are focusing on the "what" while completely ignoring the "how."
column33: We need a national shift toward Competency-Based Education (CBE). The government should mandate a higher percentage of practical work across all levels—from primary schools to universities. This includes investing in modern laboratories, vocational workshops, and mandatory industry internships. We must stop teaching subjects in a vacuum and start aligning our syllabus with the current needs of the labor market to ensure graduates are "job-ready" on day one.
